content.full_text:
August 12, 1976


Robert B. Rutter
Rutter and Davis, Accountants
1011 Second St.
Sacramento, California 95314

Dear Mr. Rutter:

Thank you for your letter of August 3 concerning the proponent's
campaign statement filed in connection with the attempt to qualify
a statewide ballot measure, the 2/3 Tax Initiative, for the June
1976 primary.

As you know, Government Code Section 84202(b) requires the propo-
nent of a proposed measure that fails to qualify for the ballot
to file a disclosure statement within sixty-five days after the
final deadline for circulating the petition, the closing date of
which is the fifty-eighth day following the deadline. You stated
that the Committee for a 2/3 Vote on Taxation received during the
reporting period a monetary contribution from the Los Angeles
Chamber of Commerce which was disclosed on the proponent's state-
ment. Since the Chamber's decision that it preferred a payment
to the Committee be reported as a "loan" rather than as a "mone-
tary contribution" was made subsequent to filing of the proponent's
statement, the Committee for a 2/3 Vote on Taxation should not
amend its report to change the nature of the contribution. To
amend the report now would distort the committee's statement of
its receipts and expenditures at the time of filing.

I hope this information is helpful. If we may be of further assis-
tance, please contact us.

Sincerely,


Nan Hambleton
Special Compliance Representative

NH:bw

Bcc: Bob Stern
     Jeanne P.

Rutter and Davis, Accountants
a Professional Corporation

August 3, 1976


Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Attention: Regina Cutchins

Dear Ms. Cutchins:

As requested, I am writing to request assistance relating to a
reporting problem I have with the Committee for a 2/3 Vote on
Taxation.

When I filed the report for the Committee, there was included a
contribution from the Los Angeles Chamber of Commerce. Subsequently, they decided that it was a loan rather than a contribution and requested that I amend the Committee's report accordingly.
In addition, they obtained contributions for the Committee to
offset the loan in part and requested that I send them a check
for the amount of the contributions raised.

My problem is, I would rather not amend the report and in order to
repay the Los Angeles Chamber of Commerce, like they want me to,
I would have to open the bank account again, deposit the checks
and then write a check to the L.A. Chamber which would close the
account again. Basically, it seems as though this entire maneuver
is meaningless and a waste of time.

Therefore, I am requesting advise as to how best to handle the above.
Please respond at your earliest convenience.

Sincerely,

Robert B. Rutter

RBR/fm


Ton Eleven Second Street · Old Sacramento, California 95814 · (916) 441-7540

sections.question: As requested, I am writing to request assistance relating to a reporting problem I have with the Committee for a 2/3 on Taxation. When I filed the report for the Committee, there was included a contribution from the Los Angeles Chamber of Commerce. Subsequently, they decided that it was a loan rather than a contribution and requested that I amend the Committee's report accordingly. In addition, they obtained contributions for the Committee to offset the loan in part and requested that I send them a check for the amount of the contributions raised. My problem is, I would rather not amend the report and in order to repay the Los Angeles Chamber of Commerce, like they want me to, I would have to open the bank account again, deposit the checks and then write a check to the L.A. Chamber which would close the account again. Basically, it seems as though this entire maneuver is meaningless and a waste of time. Therefore, I am requesting advise as to how best to handle the above.
sections.question_synthetic: The requester seeks guidance on whether a committee must amend its campaign finance report to reclassify a Los Angeles Chamber of Commerce contribution as a loan, and requests clarification on proper procedures for repaying the loan given the administrative burden involved.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request letter to FPPC, not an FPPC response letter. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections are present. Document is poorly scanned with some legibility issues but the main inquiry is clear. No explicit document ID format visible though referenced as 76ADV-318.

embedding.summary: Robert B. Rutter, an accountant for the Committee for a 2/3 on Taxation, requests FPPC advice regarding whether to amend the committee's campaign finance report to reclassify a Chamber of Commerce contribution as a loan and the proper procedure for repaying it.
